apple___black_rot.name: Apple - Black Rot
clues: Small purple spots on upper leaf surfaces that expand into 'frog-eye' lesions with light brown centers.
Black, sunken cankers on limbs and twigs which cause bark to peel.
Fruit rot starting as a brown spot, expanding into concentric circles of light and dark rings, turning black and leathery.
treatments: Prune and burn/dispose of all diseased twigs, branches, and cankers during the winter dormant season.
Remove and destroy all remaining 'mummy' fruit hanging on the trees or lying on the orchard floor.
Apply labeled copper-based fungicides or captan starting at green tip stage through summer covers.
prevention: Prune trees annually to maintain open canopy, improving air circulation and rapid leaf drying.
Keep trees vigorous through proper fertilization, watering, and pest management.
Avoid wounding bark during pruning or cultivation, which provides entry points for fungal spores.

apple___healthy.name: Apple - Healthy
clues: Leaves are uniform, deep green with strong, clean veins.
No spots, necrotic margins, white powdery coatings, or rust-colored pustules.
Fruit skin is smooth, clear, and free of corky spots, sunken lesions, or dry rotting.
treatments: No corrective fungicidal treatments required.
Apply well-rotted organic mulch to conserve soil moisture and support active soil biology.
prevention: Keep orchard floor clean of fallen autumn leaves and organic debris.
Continue routine seasonal pruning and balanced organic nutrient regimens.
Perform regular visual scouting during early spring budding.

apple___rust.name: Apple - Cedar Apple Rust
clues: Bright greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces in spring, turning brilliant orange-red.
Small raised orange spots on the underside of leaves with tiny tube-like projections (aecia).
Slightly sunken, green to brown spots on the blossom end of fruits, often bordered by a yellow halo.
treatments: Apply protective fungicides (such as Myclobutanil or Chlorothalonil) from pink bud stage through petal fall.
Remove nearby alternative host plants (galls on eastern red cedars / junipers) within a 2-mile radius if possible.
Prune out infected twigs displaying rust galls.
prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple cultivars (e.g., Liberty, Freedom, Redfree).
Do not plant apple orchards in close proximity to decorative juniper or cedar plantings.
Ensure proper row orientation to maximize morning sunlight exposure for fast canopy drying.

apple___scab.name: Apple - Scab
clues: Velvety, olive-green to brown spots on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves.
Fallen leaves in summer turn yellow and drop prematurely.
Sunken, corky, dark brown scabby lesions on fruits, causing them to crack and deform.
treatments: Spray with systemic or protective fungicides (like Captan, Flutriafol, or sulfur compounds) when green tips emerge.
Rake, chop, or dispose of fallen leaves in autumn; apply urea spray to leaves on the ground to accelerate decomposition.
prevention: Select scab-resistant cultivars such as Enterprise, Goldrush, or Prima.
Maintain wide spacing between trees to permit maximum airflow and quick leaf drying after rain.
Avoid overhead sprinkler irrigation that keeps foliage wet for prolonged periods.

blueberry___healthy.name: Blueberry - Healthy
clues: Medium to dark green waxy leaves with clean, untattered margins.
Stems are sturdy, reddish-green on new growth, without spotting or dynamic cankers.
Berries are uniform, vibrant dusty blue with no visual shriveling or grey fuzzy mold.
treatments: No pesticide or fungicide treatment needed.
Verify soil pH remains in the optimal acidic range of 4.5 to 5.2.
prevention: Apply pine needle, oak leaf, or softwood sawdust mulch to maintain soil acidity and moisture.
Prune old, unproductive canes annually to encourage vigorous new basal shoots.
Ensure adequate dripline watering during hot summer swells.

cherry___healthy.name: Cherry - Healthy
clues: Glossy, dark green leaves with no circular holes, yellowing, or web-like structures.
Branches are smooth and free of dark, gummy oozing fluid (gummosis).
Fruits are plump, glossy red-black with solid, healthy green stems.
treatments: No chemical treatments required.
Apply natural compost to support mycorrhizal soil networking.
prevention: Rake and remove cherry leaf litter in the fall.
Protect trunks from mechanical winter injury or lawnmower nicks.
Prune trees in late summer rather than winter to reduce the risk of bacterial infection.

cherry___powdery_mildew.name: Cherry - Powdery Mildew
clues: White, powdery fungal patches on the undersides of young leaves.
Leaves curl upwards, become distorted, narrow, and drop prematurely.
Infected fruit may display a fine white web of fungal mycelium, failing to develop sweetness.
treatments: Apply agricultural sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or synthetic fungicides (such as demethylation inhibitors) at first sign.
Prune infected terminal shoots in early summer to reduce inoculum load.
Keep lower branches clean of low-hanging twigs and weed growth.
prevention: Optimize air drainage when planting orchards on sloped ground.
Prune to create an open center system that allows sunlight to penetrate the inner canopy.
Avoid heavy nitrogen fertilization, which stimulates overly dense, susceptible succulent foliage.

corn___blight.name: Corn - Northern Corn Leaf Blight
clues: Long, cigar-shaped, grayish-green to tan lesions on leaves, typically 1 to 6 inches long.
Lesions appear first on lower leaves, moving upwards as the corn matures.
During wet weather, dark, dusty fungal spores appear inside the tan lesions.
treatments: Apply foliar fungicides (such as triazole or strobilurin classes) if symptoms appear before silking stage.
Chop and deeply plow crop debris post-harvest to speed up soil microbial degradation of fungal spores.
prevention: Select corn hybrids with strong genetic resistance ratings to Northern Corn Leaf Blight.
Implement a 2-year crop rotation schedule away from corn to non-host crops (e.g., soybeans).
Manage windbreak spacing to ensure fields can dry efficiently after dew or rain.

corn___gray_leaf_spot.name: Corn - Gray Leaf Spot
clues: Tan to gray, rectangular, blocky lesions strictly delimited by leaf veins, resembling matches.
Lesions merge to form large necrotic patches, leading to premature leaf death and silage loss.
Weakened stalks, making the fields prone to plants falls (lodging).
treatments: Spray recommended strobilurin-based or triazole fungicides during the V5 to R1 growth stages.
Ensure proper tillage to bury surface residue where host fungi overwinter.
prevention: Use corn hybrids with verified tolerance or resistance indicators.
Rotate out of corn for at least one season, especially under conservation or no-till practices.
Keep planting density balanced to avoid extremely high humidity levels within the crop rows.

corn___healthy.name: Corn - Healthy
clues: Leaves are broad, robust, dark green, and shoot diagonally skyward.
Stalks are thick, rigid, without spotting, borer holes, or inner rot.
Ears are filled to the tip with tight, uniform, sweet golden kernels.
treatments: No treatments required.
Ensure key macronutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium) are supplied at proper crop stages.
prevention: Keep soil aerated and avoid compaction from heavy machinery.
Conduct regular soil biochemistry testing to prevent micro-nutrient lockouts.

corn___rust.name: Corn - Common Rust
clues: Oval to elongated golden-brown pustules (uredinia) on BOTH upper and lower leaf surfaces.
Pustules rupture to release powdery, cinnamon-brown spores.
Leaves turn yellow and wither when infected under high relative humidity (over 95%).
treatments: Foliar fungicides are rarely economical on field corn but should be applied immediately on high-value sweet corn.
Apply sulfur-based powders or professional strobilurins if disease pressure is extremely high.
prevention: Plant rust-resistant corn genotypes with single dominant Rp genes.
Plant early in the season to allow crops to mature before spore populations migrate from southern areas.
Rely on natural wind and sun patterns to keep leaf surface micro-irrigation minimal.

cotton___aphids.name: Cotton - Aphids Infestation
clues: Clusters of tiny, soft-bodied yellow, green, or black insects on the undersides of leaves.
Leaves curl downward, crinkle, turn yellow, and appear deformed.
Sticky, clear fluid ('honeydew') covering leaves, often attracting black sooty mold.
treatments: Apply insecticidal soaps, neem oil extracts, or selective chemical insecticides (e.g., flonicamid).
Release natural predators such as ladybug beetles, lacewings, or parasitic wasps into the field.
prevention: Avoid excessive applications of high-nitrogen fertilizer which stimulates succulent rapid cotton growth.
Preserve existing populations of native beneficial predators by avoiding broad-spectrum synthetic sprays.
Monitor plant terminals weekly during seedling and early squaring vegetative states.

cotton___army_worm.name: Cotton - Armyworm Attack
clues: Irregular 'skeletonized' chewing holes in cotton leaves, leaving only main veins intact.
Active green to brown striped caterpillars chewing directly on squares, flowers, and bolls.
Frequent dark green, granular insect droppings (frass) gathered in leaf joints.
treatments: Spray biological larvicides of Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) when caterpillars are small.
Apply target insecticides such as Spinosad or chlorantraniliprole for advanced infestations.
prevention: Plant genetically modified Bt-cotton varieties containing insecticidal proteins.
Keep field borders clean of weeds where armyworm moths prefer to lay their eggs.
Disk oil-fields in late autumn to disrupt overwintering pupae in the topsoil.

cotton___bacterial_blight.name: Cotton - Bacterial Blight
clues: Tiny, dark green, water-soaked, angular lesions on leaves, bounded strictly by small crop veins.
Lesions turn black and progress along main leaf veins, resembling a 'black arm'.
Sunken, water-soaked, dark circular lesions on bolls, causing lint to rot and turn red-brown.
treatments: There are no effective chemical cures for bacterial blight once inside crop tissue.
Apply copper hydroxide sprays to suppress secondary spread within infected fields.
prevention: Strictly plant acid-delinted, certified disease-free cotton seeds.
Select cotton varieties with robust bacterial blight resistance genes (e.g., B2, B3, B12).
Incorporate crop residues deeply into the soil after harvest to ensure complete decay of infected tissues.

cotton___diseased.name: Cotton - General Fungus/Root Rot
clues: Sudden wilting of the whole cotton plant, especially during hot, dry afternoon weather.
Leaves turn bronze to yellow, wither, and remain attached to dead branches.
Taproot bark turns dark brown to black and sloughs off easily when pulled from the earth.
treatments: Apply premium soil-applied fungicides or bio-fungicides during seed drilling.
Immediately improve drainage in water-logged field sections to suffocate phytophthora spores.
prevention: Rotate cotton with non-susceptible grass crops (like sorghum or small grains) for 2 to 3 years.
Grow cotton on high, well-draining beds to prevent water accumulation near stem bases.
Deep plow to break hardpan layers and increase natural biological activity in the rhizosphere.

cotton___healthy.name: Cotton - Healthy
clues: Broad, vibrant green leaves with sturdy petioles and clean veins.
Robust stems with regular squares and beautiful pink-to-white blossoms.
Bolls are dry, bright white, fluffy, and clean from mold, spotting, or insect pierce stains.
treatments: No treatments required.
Monitor soil trace elements to support healthy squaring.
prevention: Maintain a steady, deep watering schedule.
Incorporate green manure into winter fallow fields.

cotton___powdery_mildew.name: Cotton - Powdery Mildew
clues: Powdery, chalky white fungal colonies on the undersides of cotton leaves.
Leaves display light yellow angular spots on upper surfaces matching the lower fungal growths.
Severe infections lead to rapid leaf death and extensive defoliation, reducing yield.
treatments: Spray with micronized dusting sulfur or systemic strobilurin fungicides.
Remove heavily infected lower leaf canopy to limit local spore wind dispersion.
prevention: Plant cotton with wider row spacing to lower inside-canopy humidity.
Avoid planting adjacent fields of wild host legumes.
Schedule early season sowing to bypass the late-summer fungal spores surges.

cotton___target_spot.name: Cotton - Target Spot
clues: Circular, dark brown lesions on lower leaves, displaying prominent concentric rings (like a target).
Lesions grow up to 1-2 cm, causing leaves to yellow and rapidly drop (defoliate).
Target spots can also appear as dark scabs on the exterior of developing bolls.
treatments: Apply protective triazole or QoI fungicides when first spots appear at the 1st to 3rd week of bloom.
Prune surrounding fields to enhance sunlight access.
prevention: Choose crop varieties with partially upright leaf canopy structures.
Rotate cotton with corn or peanuts to lower fungal inoculant in soil.
Avoid applying excess nitrogen, which creates a lush, dense, highly susceptible canopy.

grape___black_rot.name: Grape - Black Rot
clues: Small, angular, cream-colored spots with dark borders on the leaves.
Berries turn pale, then soft-brown, shriveling into hard, wrinkled black 'mummies'.
Tiny, pimple-like black fruiting bodies (pycnidia) covering shrivelled berries and leaf spots.
treatments: Spray with professional fungicides (like Myclobutanil or Strobilurins) starting at bud break until 4 weeks after bloom.
Prune and physically remove all mummified grapes from vines and trellis wires.
prevention: Train grape vines on high trellis systems to elevate canopy and maximize wind drying.
Prune out dead canes during winter dormancy and clean weed growth below vines.
Select resistant grape varieties (e.g., Norton, Chancellor) over highly susceptible ones.

grape___blight.name: Grape - Leaf Blight
clues: Large, irregular, dull-brown leaf patches starting from the margins.
Foliage becomes brittle, dry, and curls margins upward in hot summer.
Black mycelial threads visible under magnifying glass along the leaf veins.
treatments: Spray protective copper-sulfate mixes (Bordeaux mixture) or Mancozeb.
Clear fallen leaves from vineyard aisles to break the seasonal cycle.
prevention: Ensure proper vineyard site selection with slope drainage.
Avoid row spacing that crowds leaves, keeping vine density moderate.

grape___esca.name: Grape - Esca (Black Measles)
clues: Stripe-like necrosis on leaves with yellow-red margins, resembling a 'tiger-stripe' pattern.
Small, dark-purple round spots on fruit skins, causing berries to crack and dry out.
Wood cross-sections display dark necrosis dots in the center or soft yellow spongy rot.
treatments: No chemical fungicides can cure the Esca wood-rotting fungi complex once inside.
Paint large pruning wounds immediately with tree-sealant containing tebuconazole.
prevention: Avoid pruning vines during rainy or highly humid winter days when spores are airborne.
Disinfect pruning shears with 70% rubbing alcohol between vines to prevent cross-contamination.
Uproot and burn severely decayed vines showing total apoplexy/wilting.

grape___healthy.name: Grape - Healthy
clues: Leaves are broad, lobed, bright green, and free of yellow patterns.
Canes are flexible, healthy brown, showing balanced woody maturation.
Grape bunches are tight, even, plump, and covered in a natural waxy bloom.
treatments: No treatments required.
Incorporate composted grape pomace into vineyard soil to replenish potassium.
prevention: Practice clean field operations and cover crop management.
Ensure regular canopy hedging to maintain clean airflow.

orange___diseased.name: Orange - Citrus Canker / Huanglongbing
clues: Raised, corky, brown circular lesions on leaves, stems, and fruits, surrounded by yellow halos.
Mottled yellow leaf asymmetry, with shoots displaying a bright 'yellow limb' effect.
Small, bitter, misshapen, green fruits that drop prematurely with dark seed abortions.
treatments: Apply preventative copper bactericide sprays monthly to protect high-risk flush growth.
Immediately remove and burn infected trees under official citrus quarantine guidelines.
prevention: Control the Asian Citrus Psyllid vector using horticultural oils, soaps, or beneficial wasps (Tamarixia).
Establish windbreaks around citrus orchards to limit rain-blown bacterial spread.
Source certified disease-free budwood from specialized screenhouses.

peach___bacterial_spot.name: Peach - Bacterial Spot
clues: Small, angular, water-soaked, dark spots on leaves, which dry up and fall out, creating a 'shot-hole' effect.
Leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely, causing stunted tree growth.
Pitted, sunken, brown necrotic spots on the peach skin, often cracking and oozing gum.
treatments: Spray with copper bactericides or oxytetracycline in early spring starting from bud split.
Avoid using chlorothalonil or other heavy chemicals after petal fall to prevent foliage burning.
prevention: Plant bacterial spot resistant peach varieties (e.g., Redhaven, Loring, Biscoe).
Avoid planting orchard on highly sandy soil where nematodes and high wind-born sand damage occur.
Maintain optimal nitrogen fertility to help peach trees recover foliage loss.

peach___healthy.name: Peach - Healthy
clues: Long, lanceolate, rich-green leaves with smooth, glossy surfaces.
Wood is sturdy, smooth, with no active gummosis or weeping cankers.
Fruits are covered in soft, velvety peach fuzz, displaying vibrant rosy cheeks.
treatments: No treatments required.
Apply organic leaf mold mulch to feed soil microbes.
prevention: Prune annually into an open-vase center shape.
Keep orchard soil well-drained and maintain weed-free tree lanes.

pepper___bacterial_spot.name: Pepper - Bacterial Spot
clues: Small, circular, water-soaked spots on leaf undersides, turning chocolate brown with light centers.
Leaves display severe yellowing leading to rapid defoliation, exposing peppers to sunscald.
Raised, rough, blister-like circular black scabs on the pepper fruits.
treatments: Apply a tank mixture of copper hydroxide and mancozeb weekly when conditions are warm and wet.
Do NOT work in fields or cultivate crops when plants are wet with dew or rain.
prevention: Always buy certified hybrid pathogen-free seeds.
Practice a 3-year crop rotation, avoiding Solanaceous relatives (tomatoes, potatoes, eggplants).
Irrigate pepper beds using drip tape lines instead of overhead sprinklers.

pepper___healthy.name: Pepper - Healthy
clues: Leaves are lance-shaped, thick, flat, vibrant forest green.
Stems are crisp, erect, and easily bear the weight of heavy peppers.
Peppers are firm, glossy, with a uniform wax coat and thick walls.
treatments: No treatments required.
Ensure adequate calcium in soil to prevent blossom end rot.
prevention: Mulch soil to retain constant moisture.
Avoid deep cultivation that might cut shallow root systems.

potato___early_blight.name: Potato - Early Blight
clues: Dark brown, circular spots on older lower leaves with key concentric 'target' rings.
Leaves turn yellow, dry up, drop, leaving only top clusters.
Dark, sunken, dry, leathery circular lesions on potato tubers with yellow margins.
treatments: Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Pyraclostrobin at first detection.
Avoid mechanical damage to tubers during harvest to prevent fungus entry into storage.
prevention: Select early-maturing resistant potato cultivars.
Maintain excellent soil potassium levels to improve plant structural defense.
Allow potato skins to fully cure before harvesting tubers.

potato___healthy.name: Potato - Healthy
clues: Lush, bush-like, deep-green divided leaves with clean margins.
Strong white-to-purple flowers on mature growth.
Soil is loose, aerated, and harbors well-formed, clean-skinned tubers.
treatments: No chemical sprays needed.
Hill soil up around the stems to ensure greening of tubers does not occur.
prevention: Perform regular crop rotation with wheat, oats, or cover brassicas.
Ensure proper compost fertilization before planting.

potato___late_blight.name: Potato - Late Blight (Irish Famine Fungus)
clues: Large, irregular, water-soaked, dark -brown spots on leaves, rapidly expanding.
A delicate, white, downy fungal mold visible on the undersides of leaves during wet weather.
Flesh of tubers turns copper-brown and develops a smelly, wet bacterial soft rot.
treatments: Instantly spray systemics such as Metalaxyl or Cyazofamid; spray intervals must be 5-7 days under rain.
Mow and destroy potato vines 2 weeks before harvest to prevent spores from falling on soil.
prevention: Plant ONLY certified disease-free potato seed pieces.
Immediately discard and bury 'cull piles' of rotten potatoes outside the farm area.
Do not overhead irrigate in late afternoon when leaves remain wet overnight.

raspberry___healthy.name: Raspberry - Healthy
clues: Compound leaves are bright green, with pale, fuzzy undersides and sharp edges.
Canes are upright, healthy green or light brown, showing vigorous self-supporting structure.
Berries are bright ruby red, plump, separate easily from core (receptacle).
treatments: No treatments required.
Prune floricanes (second-year fruiting wood) to the ground immediately post-harvest.
prevention: Apply organic straw mulch around the rows.
Ensure wide spacing to allow deep solar penetration into the canes.

soybean___healthy.name: Soybean - Healthy
clues: Trifoliate leaves are soft green, fuzzy, broad, and uniform.
Stems are upright, bristly with rich yellow flowers or full green pods.
Roots are abundant, adorned with pink active nitrogen-fixing nodules.
treatments: No treatments required.
Ensure seed is inoculated with Rhizobium bacteria for optimal nitrogen-fixing.
prevention: Perform routine soil testing.
Rotate fields annually with corn or wheat to interrupt soil pathogens.

squash___powdery_mildew.name: Squash - Powdery Mildew
clues: Powdery, dusty, talcum-like white spots that quickly spread over both leaf surfaces.
Stems and petioles also become covered in white powdery fungal growth.
Leaves turn yellow, brown, shrivel, and die, resulting in stunted squash growth and sunscalded fruit.
treatments: Spray with neem oil, horticultural mineral oils, potassium bicarbonate, or sulfur fungicides.
Remove heavily infected older leaves to increase light penetration.
prevention: Plant squash in locations with maximum daily sunlight and dry soil drainage.
Choose powdery mildew resistant squash hybrids (e.g., Goldbar, sungold).
Avoid overhead sprinkler irrigation; always irrigate the crop roots directly.

strawberry___healthy.name: Strawberry - Healthy
clues: Deep green, three-part glossy leaves with beautiful serrated margins.
Crown is firm, set flush with soil surface, throwing strong, healthy runners.
Berries are uniform, glossy red, with bright yellow-green seeds (achenes) on surface.
treatments: No chemical actions needed.
Mulch clean pine needles or clean straw under the foliage to keep berries off bare mud.
prevention: Renovate strawberry beds annually to maintain healthy, young runner plants.
Remove weeds continuously to keep field humidity low.

strawberry___scorch.name: Strawberry - Leaf Scorch
clues: Numerous small, dark purple spots on leaves that expand and merge into large purple blotches.
Centers of lesions remain purple, unlike leaf spot (which has tan centers).
Leaves turn bright red, dry up, brown, look scorched or burned, then die.
treatments: Apply protective fungicides (like Thiophanate-methyl or Captan) before blooms and after harvest.
Mow and renovate beds immediately after harvest to destroy old, spore-harboring leaves.
prevention: Avoid over-fertilization with nitrogen in early spring, which makes leaves soft and sensitive.
Select scorched resistant cultivars such as Allstar, Earliglow, or Guardian.
Plant strawberries in well-drained, raised soil mounds.

tomato___bacterial_spot.name: Tomato - Bacterial Spot
clues: Small, angular, water-soaked, dark-brown spots on leaves, turning greasy with yellow halos.
Foliage dries up, becomes brittle, and drops, causing heavy crop defoliation.
Small, dark, raised, scabby, blister-like spots on tomato skins.
treatments: Spray copper formulations mixed with Mancozeb weekly starting at transplanting.
Sanitize tomato stakes, cages, and tools using 10% bleach solutions between seasons.
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seeds or healthy nursery transplants.
Implement a 3-year crop rotation away from peppers, eggplants, and potatoes.
Never harvest or prune tomatoes when leaves are wet from dew or localized rains.

tomato___early_blight.name: Tomato - Early Blight
clues: Irregular dark brown spots on older lower leaves with prominent concentric rings (target pattern).
The leaf tissue surrounding the spots turns bright yellow, eventually dropping off.
Leathery black, sunken, concentric circle lesions on the stem near the soil line.
treatments: Prune and strip off the bottom 12 inches of foliage after blossoms arrive to prevent soil splash.
Spray preventive copper fungicides, Chlorothalonil, or organic Serenade biofungicide.
prevention: Mulch soil heavily with straw, shredded leaves, or plastic to block soil-borne spores.
Maintain a 3-foot spacing between tomato bushes to enhance airflow.
Support tomatoes off the ground using sturdy stakes, cages, or trellis lines.

tomato___healthy.name: Tomato - Healthy
clues: Leaves are lush, divided, deep forest green, and smell wonderfully herbaceous.
Stems are hairy, thick, rigid, displaying many yellow flowers and strong growth hubs.
Tomatoes are firm, uniform, deep glossy red, free of cracks, scabs, or water spots.
treatments: No remedial action required.
Apply high-potassium organic fertilizer during active fruit set stages.
prevention: Maintain deep, uniform, scheduled soil watering to avoid skin cracking.
Ensure consistent cage support as fruit weights increase.

tomato___late_blight.name: Tomato - Late Blight
clues: Large, fast-growing, water-soaked, dark-gray lesions on leaves, turning greasy.
A white, velvety, downy fungal growth on leaf undersides in humid mornings.
Large, firm, greasy-looking, chocolate-brown blotches on tomato fruits.
treatments: Apply systemic late-blight fungicides immediately (e.g., Mandipropamid, Famoxadone).
Pull up, bag, compost-bury, or burn infected vines completely; do NOT leave on soil.
prevention: Grow late-blight resistant tomato cultivars (e.g., Mountain Merit, Iron Lady, Defiant).
Keep dynamic wind barriers open to allow sun drying.
Inspect transplant leaves continuously under magnifying light.

tomato___leaf_mold.name: Tomato - Leaf Mold
clues: Pale green-to-yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces, with olive-green velvety molds underneath.
Foliage becomes yellow, curls up, dies, and drops when grown in high humidity (>85%).
Fungal growth starting on oldest leaves, moving rapidly up into the greenhouse rafters.
treatments: Apply copper fungicides or sulfur sprays at first detection under foliage canopy.
Increase greenhouse ventilation by adding positive exhaust fans to decrease air moisture.
prevention: Grow leaf-mold resistant cultivars (e.g., Geronimo, Sakura).
Prune sucker branches and lower foliage to create draft tunnels under crop rows.
Keep heating vents active in greenhouses to prevent nighttime condensation.

tomato___mosaic_virus.name: Tomato - Mosaic Virus
clues: Striking light and dark green mottled 'mosaic' or 'fern-leaf' patterns on leaves.
Leaves are severely narrowed, twisted, stunted, resembling shoestrings.
Fruits are small, with interior brown necrotic rings and dull skin colors.
treatments: There is no cure or chemical spray treatment for plant viruses once infected.
Immediately uproot and burn/bury infected plants; wash hands of all tobacco residues.
prevention: Wash hands and sterilize all gardening tools with nonfat dry milk or disinfectants.
Plant mosaic resistant hybrid seed varieties (marked with TMV or ToMV).
Control weed hosts and sucking insect pests (e.g., Thrips, Aphids) that transmit virions.

tomato___septoria.name: Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot
clues: Numerous small, circular spots on leaves with dark borders and grey/tan centers.
Tiny black specks (pycnidia) can be seen clearly within the tan centers.
Starts on lowest leaves and progresses rapidly upward, causing total defoliation.
treatments: Apply protective fungicides (e.g., Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb) at the first trace of spots.
Remove and destroy infected crop residues after harvest; never till them in place.
prevention: Practice strict 3-year crop rotation of all Solanaceous family vegetable crops.
Apply deep organic mulches to create physical shields blocking soil spores.
Enforce bottom-watering using drip lines only; avoid overhead sprinklers.

tomato___spider_mites.name: Tomato - Two-Spotted Spider Mites
clues: Fine, yellow-white speckling or stippling on the upper leaf surfaces.
Fine, silken web structures on the undersides of leaves, stems, and fruits.
Leaves turn dusty brown, dry up, drop, showing severe leaf bronzing.
treatments: Apply insecticidal soaps, horticultural oils, neem extracts, or specific miticides (e.g., abamectin).
Spray plants with strong water streams to dislodge mites and wash off web shelters.
prevention: Preserve natural beneficial mites (e.g., Phytoseiidae) or release ladybugs.
Keep dusty soil roads sprayed with water; mites reproduce faster in dry, dusty environments.
Ensure tomatoes are well-watered, as water-stressed crops are more susceptible.

tomato___target_spot.name: Tomato - Target Spot
clues: Small, pinpoint, water-soaked spots on leaves expanding into circular brown lesions.
Lesions display concentric rings (resembling a target) with light green halos.
Fruits get slightly sunken, circular lesions with dark brown, velvety cracks in centers.
treatments: Spray with protectant Triazole fungicides at early bloom or first symptom onset.
Prune plants to single stems to increase air movement and direct sunlight drying.
prevention: Select target-spot tolerant varieties.
Keep soil mulch barrier thick to block spores splashing up from dirt.
Ensure thorough crop rotation sequence with grass cover or cruciferous fallows.

tomato___yellow_leaf_curl.name: Tomato - Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV)
clues: Severe stunting of new tomato growth, with small, pale green shoots.
Leaves curl dramatically upward and inward, resembling small cups.
Leaf margins turn bright yellow; flowers fall off prematurely, stopping fruit set.
treatments: No chemical sprays can cure the TYLCV virus once a tomato is infected.
Uproot and destroy infected plants immediately to stop secondary whitefly vectors.
prevention: Control silverleaf whitefly vectors using systemic imidacloprid or yellow sticky traps.
Cover young plants with specialized insect exclusion netting until blooming.
SelectTYLCV-resistant hybrid tomato seed lines (look for the TY rating).
